itemListElement.name: AJ20D6 / DT306 (3.0L Ingenium I6 Twin-Turbo Diesel MHEV)

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Sport D350 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned D350 3.0-litre Ingenium diesel engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £3,500–£5,500. Fitting adds £1,300–£2,500 depending on your location and workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Range Rover Sport.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Sport D350 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The D350 is powered by JLR's 3.0-litre Ingenium straight-six diesel — a meaningfully more robust engine than the troubled 2.0-litre diesel. However, it has known issues: front engine cover oil leaks (a known JLR warranty pattern), timing chain stretch (oil dilution from DPF regeneration is a contributing factor), DPF clogging (urban use), and EGR cooler failures. Regular maintenance (12,000-mile oil changes with 0W-30 JLR-spec oil) is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover Sport D350?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are front engine cover oil leaks (a known warranty pattern affecting Defenders, Discoveries and Range Rovers with the Ingenium engine), timing chain stretch (accelerated by oil dilution from DPF regeneration), DPF clogging (short urban journeys), and EGR cooler failures. The 3.0-litre is less prone to these issues than the 2.0-litre, but vigilance is still required.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover Sport D350 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £60,000–£90,000 Range Rover Sport, a £7,000 recon is 8–12% of the car's value — definitely worth it. Against a high-mileage £35,000 example, the same recon approaches our 60% Land Rover threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover Sport D350 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The D350 carries the engine code AJ20D6 (also referred to as DT306 in some documentation). It's a 3.0-litre straight-six twin-turbo diesel with a 48V mild-hybrid system, used in the L494 (2020–2022) and L461 (2022–present) Range Rover Sport.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain and tensioner replacement costs £1,200–£2,500. Oil dilution from failed DPF regenerations is a key contributor to timing chain wear. If ignored and the chain snaps, the engine is an interference design — pistons and valves collide, resulting in catastrophic engine damage. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes using 0W-30 JLR-spec oil are the single most effective preventative measure.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Sport D350 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the AJ20D6 3.0-litre Ingenium diesel. Most Range Rover Sport engine replacements range between £4,500 and £12,000 depending on engine type and specification.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover Sport D350 engine typically takes 15–25 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare D350 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Front Engine Cover Oil Leak
problems.symptoms: Oil spots on the garage floor, oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning, visible oil residue around the front of the engine.
problems.typicalMileage: 15,000–30,000 miles (can occur on nearly new vehicles)
problems.repairCost: £800–£1,500 (front cover + crank seal replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: This is a known JLR warranty issue affecting Defenders, Discoveries and Range Rovers with the Ingenium diesel. The remedy involves replacing the front engine cover and front crank seal. If your D350 is exhibiting oil leaks, have it inspected promptly — while not catastrophic if caught early, ignoring it can lead to low oil levels and potential engine damage. The repair, once done in a workshop rather than on the production line, is reportedly better than when it came out of the factory.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch & Oil Dilution
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, DPF filling prematurely.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (chain + tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (if chain skips and valves contact pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Oil dilution from failed DPF regeneration is a key contributor to lubrication degradation in Ingenium engines, accelerating timing chain wear. Stretched timing chains can cause the DPF to fill prematurely, creating a cascade of failures. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain and tensioners inspected immediately. Regular oil changes using the correct 0W-30 spec oil are the single most effective preventative measure.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: DPF Clogging & EGR Cooler Failures
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced performance, increased fuel consumption, failed regeneration cycles, white smoke from exhaust.
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 (forced regeneration, DPF clean, or EGR cooler replacement)
problems.recommendation: The Ingenium diesel's DPF is sensitive to short urban journeys. Cracked EGR coolers can allow coolant to enter the combustion system. If you predominantly drive in town, ensure the vehicle completes regular regeneration cycles — a motorway run at 2,000+ rpm for 20 minutes at least once a week is recommended. The 3.0-litre is less prone to these issues than the 2.0-litre, but they remain a known pattern.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover Sport D350 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Warranty-covered on many examples; straightforward fix
Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Forced regeneration or DPF clean — less than replacement
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This D350? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented front engine cover inspection or replacement (warranty work completed)
* Full service history with 0W-30 JLR-spec oil at 12,000-mile (or less) intervals
* Evidence of DPF-friendly driving — regular motorway runs
* No cold-start rattle — a quiet startup suggests healthy timing chain
avoidIf: * Oil leaks with no documented front cover repair history
* No proof of regular oil changes — the Ingenium diesel is unforgiving of neglect
* DPF warning light with no regeneration history
* Engine warning light with timing, EGR, or DPF codes and no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The D350 is the high-output diesel in the Range Rover Sport line-up, powered by JLR's 3.0-litre Ingenium straight-six. It's a meaningfully more robust engine than the troubled 2.0-litre diesel, but it's still a modern diesel with modern diesel problems — front-cover oil leaks, timing chain vigilance, and DPF/EGR maintenance are the price of entry. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained D350 is a £60,000–£90,000 luxury SUV; a neglected one is a £7,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare D350 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Sport D350 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or metallic ticking on startup (timing chain wear warning)
* Service History — including the D350 era and confirmed oil-change intervals using 0W-30 JLR-spec oil
* Oil Leak Check — Any history of oil leaks? (front engine cover is a known weak point)
* DPF Check — Check for DPF warning light or evidence of regeneration issues (urban-driven vehicles are high risk)
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate EGR coolant leak
* Oil Level — Check the dipstick; any evidence of oil dilution from failed DPF regenerations?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, EGR, DPF, or injector issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, or injector-related)
